title: Day 1 · Phase 1 — Re-anchoring: what if it hits, and could we nudge it?
bodyHtml:
DAY 1 · Phase 1 — From orbits to collisions

Unit 2 told us WHERE 2026-XJ is going. Unit 3 asks: what happens if Earth is in the way — and is there anything we could do about it?

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS (preview)

K1. Momentum as a measure of "motion that's hard to stop." R3. Why deflecting an asteroid is a momentum problem (preview).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Where we are: end of Unit 2, students computed closest approach. The closest approach number is uncomfortably close.

Today's pivot: Two questions Unit 3 will answer. (1) If 2026-XJ collides with Earth, what's the physics that decides what happens? (2) NASA actually deflected an asteroid in 2022 (DART mission). What did they do, and would it work for our asteroid?

Lesson Flow

(Assumes a ~50-minute period; adjust for block schedule.)

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Return Unit 2 transfer tasks. Highlight any group that got the asteroid closest-approach reasonable. "You know where it's going. Now: what if Earth is in the way?"
5–18 min

• Demo: roll a small Hot Wheels car gently into a cardboard wall — nothing happens. Roll the same car FAST — wall buckles. Then take a HEAVIER cart at the slow speed — wall buckles. Ask: "What MAKES a moving thing hard to stop?"
18–28 min

• Notice & Wonder. Lead the class to name TWO things that matter: how MUCH stuff is moving (mass) and how FAST it's moving (velocity). Tease: "There's a single number that combines both. We call it momentum."
28–42 min

• Mini-lecture preview: Unit 3 has two big payoffs. One: what 2026-XJ's IMPACT looks like in physics terms (Days 12–13). Two: the 2022 DART mission, where NASA bumped an asteroid by a few cm/s and changed its orbit. Show a 30-second DART animation if available.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Between (a) a 1500-kg car at 30 m/s and (b) a 0.05-kg bullet at 400 m/s, which would be HARDER to stop with your hand? Why?" (Both are honest answers; force students to name the trade-off.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1/R3 preview.

Prep: Unit 2 transfer tasks scored; Hot Wheels car + a heavier cart (or block + tape); cardboard or thin foam target; clear floor zone; DART mission animation bookmarked.

Talk moves on the exit ticket: there's no clean "right" answer — that's the point. Push: "Mass-only or speed-only doesn't capture it. What does?" Let them want a combined number.

Differentiation: ELs — sentence frame "_____ would be harder because _____"; SWDs — verbal version of exit ticket offered; advanced — "if we made a number 'mass times velocity,' what would the units be?"

title: Day 2 · Phase 1 — Momentum p = mv
bodyHtml:
DAY 2 · Phase 1 — Defining the quantity

p = mv. Units kg·m/s. Vector — direction matters.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K1. Momentum definition + vector nature. S1. Compute momentum.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

What's 2026-XJ's momentum? Big number. Numbing big. Today we build the scale of "big-momentum object" vs. "small-momentum object" so the asteroid math lands later.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hand back Day 1 exit tickets. Lift any student wording that's close to "combine mass and speed." Anchor: today's the day we define it.
5–15 min

• Direct instruction: p = mv. Units: kg·m/s. Vector — same direction as velocity. Write it big on the board.
15–30 min

• Worked example sequence: (1) 1500-kg car at 30 m/s east. (2) 0.05-kg bullet at 400 m/s east. (3) 70-kg sprinter at 10 m/s east. (4) 5×10⁶-kg cruise ship at 8 m/s east. (5) 2026-XJ at its current speed — pick a sensible asteroid mass + velocity. Put the five momenta side-by-side. Magnitudes wildly different.
30–42 min

• Quick practice — packet has 6 problems mixing mass + velocity, including a couple where direction matters (vector signs).
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.15-kg baseball moving at 40 m/s east meets the bat. After it bounces back at 50 m/s WEST, what is the CHANGE in its momentum?" (Vector subtraction; force the sign.)
48–50 min

• Marzano on K1/S1.

Prep: packet problems printed; calculator availability; the five-example momentum table pre-drawn on the board.

Talk moves on the baseball exit ticket: students will subtract the magnitudes. Push: "What direction is positive? What direction is the final velocity? What's the actual sign of v_f?"

Differentiation: ELs — provide a units-only example (kg × m/s = kg·m/s); SWDs — pre-printed table for the 5 worked examples; advanced — "compute momentum of Earth (6×10²⁴ kg) moving at 30 km/s around the Sun."

title: Day 3 · Phase 1 — Impulse + impulse-momentum theorem
bodyHtml:
DAY 3 · Phase 1 — How forces CHANGE momentum

J = FΔt = Δp. The bridge between force (Unit 1) and momentum (Unit 3).

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K2. Impulse-momentum theorem. S2. Compute impulse and Δp.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

To CHANGE 2026-XJ's momentum we need to apply a force OVER A TIME. The DART mission did exactly that — a small force, applied briefly, changed asteroid momentum by a tiny but measurable amount. Today's math IS the DART math.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: write p = mv. Units? Vector or scalar?
5–18 min

• Derivation move: start from F = ma = m·Δv/Δt. Multiply both sides by Δt: FΔt = mΔv = Δp. Name FΔt as impulse (J). Units: N·s = kg·m/s (same as momentum).
18–30 min

• Worked example: 0.15-kg baseball changes from +40 m/s to -50 m/s. Δp = ? If the bat-ball contact lasts 0.002 s, what FORCE did the bat apply?
30–42 min

• Group practice — 5 problems mixing knowns: sometimes you know F and Δt, find Δp. Sometimes you know Δp and Δt, find F.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "DART hit its asteroid for 0.05 s with an average force of 8×10⁵ N. What change in asteroid momentum did it deliver?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K2/S2. Tomorrow: WHY does impulse matter beyond the math?

Prep: packet practice problems; calculator availability; the J = FΔt = Δp triangle on the board.

Talk moves on the derivation: watch for students who don't see WHY FΔt equals Δp. Stop. "Where in F = ma is there a time? Where is there a velocity change?" Have them point.

Differentiation: ELs — vocab card (impulse / impulso, momentum / cantidad de movimiento); SWDs — pre-printed Δp = p_f − p_i column for each problem; advanced — "impulse equals area under an F-vs-t graph. Why?" (Calculus preview.)

title: Day 4 · Phase 1 — Why impulse matters: airbags, follow-through, padding
bodyHtml:
DAY 4 · Phase 1 — Real-world impulse

To get the SAME Δp you can use BIG force / SHORT time, or SMALL force / LONG time. That choice is everywhere.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R1. Reason about how varying Δt (or F) achieves a given Δp.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

To deflect 2026-XJ we want the LARGEST Δp possible. So we want the longest contact time AND/OR the biggest force. DART did short-and-hard. Other concepts (gravity tractor, ion engine push) do long-and-soft.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: drop an egg onto concrete (don't actually — show video). Then drop one into a pillow. Same Δp. Different F. Why?
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: J = FΔt. For a fixed Δp, FΔt is FIXED. Bigger Δt → smaller F. Bigger Δt → safer. Airbags. Crumple zones. Follow-through in baseball/tennis (extends contact). Padded floors in gyms.
18–32 min

• Group case study analysis — pairs pick one of: airbag, helmet, boxing glove, gymnastics mat, car crumple zone, parkour roll. Articulate the SAME Δp / longer Δt / smaller F logic for their scenario.
32–42 min

• Worked example: 80-kg sprinter going 9 m/s stops in 0.05 s (concrete) vs. 0.5 s (foam pit). Compute F for each. Show the 10× difference.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A boxer 'rolls with the punch.' Why does this hurt LESS for the same Δp?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R1.

Prep: short video clips (egg + pillow, car crash test with vs. without airbag); case study cards printed; calculator availability.

Talk moves on "safer" reasoning: students may say "bigger Δt means less momentum." Stop. "Did the momentum CHANGE? Or did the FORCE delivering the change change?"

Differentiation: ELs — visual pairs (no padding/padded) with arrows showing Δt; SWDs — pre-built table for the case study with two columns (situation / impulse logic); advanced — "design an asteroid deflection that uses long Δt instead of short. What would it look like?"

title: Day 5 · Phase 2 — Conservation of momentum: explosions + recoil
bodyHtml:
DAY 5 · Phase 2 — Total momentum stays the same

Σp_before = Σp_after. No external force means momentum's conserved.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K3. State conservation of momentum + its condition (no net external force).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

When DART hit its target, total momentum BEFORE = total momentum AFTER. The asteroid sped up because the spacecraft slowed down (and got destroyed). Same logic, asteroid-scale.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–3 min

• Quick recap: J = Δp. What gives a Δp? External force × time.
3–15 min

• The flip: if NO external force acts on a SYSTEM, total p of the system doesn't change. Demo: two carts (different masses) at rest with a compressed spring between, released. They recoil. Σp_before = 0. Σp_after = m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = 0. The smaller cart moves faster.
15–28 min

• Worked example: 70-kg ice skater throws a 5-kg ball at 8 m/s east. Skater recoils west at what speed? Show the algebra.
28–40 min

• Group practice — 4 explosion/recoil problems. Mix masses + velocities. Include a rocket-style problem (continuous case treated as before/after).
40–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.5-kg firework explodes at rest into two pieces: a 0.3-kg piece moving east at 12 m/s, and a 0.2-kg piece moving — what direction and how fast?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K3.

Prep: two carts + spring (or two skateboards + students if no carts); practice problems printed; calculator availability.

Talk moves on the cart demo: after the carts recoil, ask: "What was the total momentum BEFORE? What's the total momentum AFTER? How do those numbers compare? Why?" Push them to articulate the rule.

Differentiation: ELs — diagram with arrows for each object's velocity, before/after; SWDs — fill-in-the-blank conservation equation with placeholders; advanced — "a rocket continuously expels exhaust. How does conservation of momentum explain thrust?"

title: Day 6 · Phase 2 — Collisions: elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic
bodyHtml:
DAY 6 · Phase 2 — Three flavors of collision

Momentum's always conserved. Energy's only conserved in elastic ones. Stick-together = perfectly inelastic.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

K4. Distinguish elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic collisions.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid striking Earth would be PERFECTLY INELASTIC — the asteroid effectively becomes part of Earth (after vaporizing). A glancing flyby with no contact would be ELASTIC (gravity scattering only). DART was somewhere in between — momentum delivered, but the spacecraft was destroyed.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick check: conservation of momentum rule + condition.
5–18 min

• Three categories on the board: Elastic (bounce, no KE loss), Inelastic (bounce, some KE lost), Perfectly inelastic (stick together, max KE lost). All three: momentum IS conserved.
18–32 min

• Demos: superball off concrete = nearly elastic. Clay ball off concrete = inelastic. Velcro carts colliding and sticking = perfectly inelastic. After each, ask: "Where did the energy go in this case?"
32–42 min

• Vocabulary practice: classify 8 real-world collisions (car crash, billiard balls, football tackle, baseball + bat, asteroid impact, two magnets repelling without contact, etc.). Pair-share.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A meteorite buries itself in the desert. What kind of collision is this? Where did its kinetic energy go?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on K4. Tomorrow: Vernier collision lab.

Prep: superball, clay ball, Velcro carts (or carts + masking tape), concrete or hard tile surface, classification worksheet.

Talk moves on the demo: after the clay ball, ask: "Why DIDN'T the clay bounce? What changed?" Push toward energy lost to deformation/heat.

Differentiation: ELs — three-column visual (elastic / inelastic / perfectly inelastic) with one example each; SWDs — fewer classification items, more partner discussion; advanced — "is any real collision PERFECTLY elastic? When is the approximation good?"

title: Day 7 · Phase 2 — Vernier collision lab: verify conservation
bodyHtml:
DAY 7 · Phase 2 — Measure Σp before and after

Two Vernier carts. Two photogates. Run elastic and inelastic collisions. Compare Σp_before to Σp_after.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S4. Vernier collision measurement. K3. Conservation of momentum, experimental confirmation.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If conservation of momentum holds in our lab carts, we can TRUST it for asteroids too. Today's lab is the empirical foundation for the asteroid math on Days 11–13.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Lab procedure overview. Safety: tracks level, carts at gentle pushes only, photogates clamped.
5–18 min

• Teacher demo: one elastic collision (magnet-bumpered carts) — read v_before and v_after off photogates. Compute Σp_before and Σp_after. Show they match.
18–38 min

• Lab: pairs run 2 trials of an elastic collision (magnets) and 2 of a perfectly inelastic collision (Velcro). Record v_before and v_after for each cart. Compute Σp_before and Σp_after. Compare.
38–45 min

• Class data compilation: % difference between Σp_before and Σp_after across all groups. Discuss sources of error (friction on track, photogate timing, mass measurement).
45–48 min

• Lab write-up start (data + claim-evidence-reasoning paragraph). Homework finish.
48–50 min

• Marzano on S4.

Prep: Vernier dynamics carts + tracks + magnet bumpers + Velcro pads; photogates + LabQuest interfaces; balance for cart masses; lab write-up template.

Talk moves on the % difference: if a group is at 8% off, push: "What in the setup leaked momentum out of your system? Was the track tilted? Did the cart drag?"

Differentiation: ELs — partner with a LoggerPro-fluent student; SWDs — pre-assigned roles (driver / scribe / equation-checker); advanced — "the elastic collision lost SOME momentum on paper. Where? Estimate the missing % from track friction."

title: Day 8 · Phase 2 — Inelastic collision math: stick-together problems
bodyHtml:
DAY 8 · Phase 2 — Perfectly inelastic worked examples

m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁ + m₂) v_f. The clean case.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S3. Solve perfectly inelastic 1D collision problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid impact is the planetary version of "stick together and continue." Today's math is exactly the framework we'd use to estimate Earth-system velocity change from a 2026-XJ impact.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick recap of yesterday's lab. Did the inelastic trials confirm conservation? Within how much %?
5–15 min

• Set up the inelastic equation: m₁v₁ + m₂v₂ = (m₁ + m₂) v_f. Walk through it as a special case of total conservation.
15–28 min

• Worked example 1: classic ballistic pendulum — 0.05-kg bullet at 400 m/s into a 5-kg block at rest. Find v_f of bullet-plus-block.
28–40 min

• Worked example 2: two cars colliding head-on and locking together (one east, one west). Find final velocity. Force direction reasoning.
40–46 min

• Quick practice — 3 problems on a worksheet.
46–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 1000-kg car at 20 m/s east rear-ends a 2000-kg stalled truck. They lock. What's the final velocity?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S3.

Prep: packet worksheet; calculators; ballistic pendulum image or video.

Talk moves on the head-on case: when students forget to use signs for opposite directions, stop. "What direction is east? West? What sign does each velocity have?"

Differentiation: ELs — solved equation template with each variable labeled; SWDs — Step-1/Step-2 worksheet structure; advanced — "compute the KE lost in the head-on collision. Where did it go?"

title: Day 9 · Phase 2 — Elastic collision math: bounce-apart problems
bodyHtml:
DAY 9 · Phase 2 — Elastic worked examples

Both momentum AND kinetic energy conserved. Two equations, two unknowns.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

S3. Solve 1D elastic collision problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

A flyby with no contact is essentially elastic — gravity scatters the asteroid, momentum and energy both conserved. The math here is what we'd use to predict an asteroid's path AFTER a gravity-assist swing-by.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Quick recap: elastic = both p and KE conserved.
5–18 min

• Set up: two equations. Σp: m₁v₁ᵢ + m₂v₂ᵢ = m₁v₁f + m₂v₂f. ΣKE: ½m₁v₁ᵢ² + ½m₂v₂ᵢ² = ½m₁v₁f² + ½m₂v₂f². Two unknowns (v₁f, v₂f). Solvable.
18–32 min

• Worked example: 0.5-kg ball at 4 m/s strikes a 1.0-kg ball at rest elastically. Find both final velocities. (Use the simplified result for one-at-rest: v₁f = ((m₁−m₂)/(m₁+m₂))v₁ᵢ; v₂f = (2m₁/(m₁+m₂))v₁ᵢ.)
32–42 min

• Pattern-finding: what happens when m₁ = m₂? When m₁ ≫ m₂? When m₁ ≪ m₂? Use the simplified formulas to predict, then check with PhET Collision Lab.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "A 0.2-kg superball at 5 m/s east strikes a 0.2-kg ball at rest, elastically. What happens to each?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on S3.

Prep: PhET Collision Lab on lab computers; simplified-formula reference card; calculator.

Talk moves on the equal-mass case: after the formulas predict v₁f=0, v₂f=v₁ᵢ, push: "Newton's cradle. What just happened? Does that match the result?"

Differentiation: ELs — formula card with each symbol labeled; SWDs — students can use the simplified formulas without re-deriving; advanced — "derive the simplified one-at-rest formulas from the two conservation equations."

title: Day 10 · Phase 3 — 2D collisions: glancing blows
bodyHtml:
DAY 10 · Phase 3 — Two dimensions, vector momentum

Conservation applies COMPONENT-WISE: Σpₓ before = Σpₓ after. Same for y.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R2. Apply 2D conservation of momentum to glancing collisions.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

An asteroid that's NUDGED doesn't move in a straight line afterward — it deflects sideways. 2D momentum is the framework for asking "how much sideways?" Critical for DART-style deflection analysis tomorrow.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Hook: billiard balls. A cue ball strikes a stationary ball at an angle. Both move off in different directions. Both p_x and p_y must balance.
5–18 min

• Direct instruction: vector momentum p has p_x and p_y components. Conservation applies SEPARATELY to each: Σpₓ_before = Σpₓ_after; Σpᵧ_before = Σpᵧ_after. Two equations.
18–32 min

• Worked example: 0.16-kg cue ball at 2 m/s east strikes a 0.16-kg stationary ball. After: cue ball goes 1 m/s at 30° north of east. What's the second ball's velocity (magnitude + direction)?
32–42 min

• Practice — 3 problems on PhET Collision Lab in 2D mode. Vary angles. Predict, then check.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why must p_x AND p_y each be conserved separately, not just total p?"
48–50 min

• Marzano on R2.

Prep: PhET Collision Lab with 2D mode enabled; billiards image; vector decomposition reference from Unit 1.

Talk moves on the exit ticket: push past "because they're separate." Ask: "What would happen if p_x balanced but p_y didn't? What does that mean physically?"

Differentiation: ELs — pre-drawn coordinate grid with vectors labeled; SWDs — partner with computational-fluent student for the worked example; advanced — "what's the maximum deflection angle a cue ball can have when striking an equal-mass ball at rest, elastically?"

title: Day 11 · Phase 3 — DART mission case study: nudging an asteroid
bodyHtml:
DAY 11 · Phase 3 — A real spacecraft, a real nudge

NASA's DART crashed into Dimorphos in 2022 and changed its orbital period by 32 minutes.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R3. Reason about asteroid deflection using momentum + impulse.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

This is the day we ask: COULD we deflect 2026-XJ? If DART changed Dimorphos's orbit measurably, what would it take to do the same to our asteroid? The unit's been building to this question.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief: who knows about DART? Show a 1-minute mission animation.
5–18 min

• Case study direct instruction: DART was ~600 kg, hit Dimorphos (~5×10⁹ kg) at ~6 km/s. Use conservation of momentum (perfectly inelastic) to estimate the velocity change Δv given to the asteroid.
18–32 min

• Compute Δv. (Will be a few mm/s.) Discuss: that's TINY. Why does it matter? Because it accumulates over years — the asteroid's POSITION drifts further off course every orbit.
32–44 min

• Group problem: 2026-XJ has a mass of (give a value, maybe 10⁹ kg) and a closest-approach trajectory bringing it within (say 50,000 km of Earth). What Δv would deflect it by an Earth-radius offset, given (say 6 months) lead time? Run the numbers.
44–48 min

• Discussion: is the required Δv DART-scale or much bigger? What does this tell us about real planetary defense?
48–50 min

• Marzano on R3.

Prep: DART mission animation bookmarked; case study card with DART numbers; calculator availability; whiteboard space.

Talk moves on the tiny Δv: when students balk at "3 mm/s," push: "Multiply that by how many seconds are in a year. How many kilometers off course does that put it?"

Differentiation: ELs — provide the DART numbers with units pre-labeled; SWDs — pre-built calculation worksheet with steps; advanced — "what if DART had been twice as fast? Would Δv be twice or four times bigger?" (Linear in speed.)

title: Day 12 · Phase 4 — Asteroid impact: what momentum says about damage
bodyHtml:
DAY 12 · Phase 4 — The other scenario — what if it hits

If deflection fails, what does the impact look like in physics terms?

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

R2/R3. Apply momentum and impulse to estimate impact effects (preview to Unit 4 energy).

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

If 2026-XJ hits Earth, momentum is the FIRST framework we'd use. Earth's velocity changes by (almost nothing — because Earth's mass is colossally larger). But the asteroid's momentum is delivered to a small impact zone over a short time — that's the impulse that does the damage.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame: "Today we play out the other branch. Deflection fails. It hits."
5–15 min

• Use Unit 2's velocity prediction + a reasonable 2026-XJ mass. Compute the asteroid's momentum at impact.
15–30 min

• Apply impulse-momentum theorem to the impact: contact time ≈ 0.1 s (asteroid stops). What's the AVERAGE force? Convert to a meaningful unit (compare to a building, an earthquake).
30–42 min

• Class discussion: what does this LARGE force not capture? (Energy. Heat. Shockwave.) Tease Unit 4: kinetic energy at impact gives a more useful damage estimate. Today's number is one PART of the picture.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Why is Earth's velocity change after an asteroid impact essentially zero, even though the impact is catastrophic locally?"
48–50 min

• Marzano.

Prep: 2026-XJ parameter card (mass, impact velocity from Unit 2); calculator; reference for "how big is a Newton" (1 N ≈ apple weight).

Talk moves on Earth's negligible Δv: push: "Earth's mass is 10²⁴ kg. The asteroid's mass is 10⁹ kg. Look at the ratio. Now look at the velocity Earth gains."

Differentiation: ELs — equation laid out with each term labeled; SWDs — provide the numbers on a step-card; advanced — "if the impact angle is 30° from vertical, how does that change the momentum delivered HORIZONTALLY?"

title: Day 13 · Phase 4 — Putting it together: 2026-XJ momentum analysis
bodyHtml:
DAY 13 · Phase 4 — The unit's payoff day

One scenario, two branches. Combine everything from Days 1–12.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 3 targets surfaced through one complete analysis.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

Today the class completes a full "what should we do?" analysis for 2026-XJ using only Unit 3 tools. Tomorrow is the transfer task.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Frame the day. "Use Unit 3 thinking on the asteroid. Two branches: deflect or impact. We'll do both."
5–18 min

• Group station 1 — DEFLECTION. Given 2026-XJ parameters + a notional spacecraft, compute the Δv DART-style delivery would produce. Then: how many DARTs (or how much bigger) to actually deflect by an Earth-radius?
18–32 min

• Group station 2 — IMPACT. Given the asteroid's momentum at strike, compute average force during 0.1-s impact contact. Compare to known disasters.
32–42 min

• Whole-class compilation: each group reports. Build a class consensus prediction.
42–48 min

• Exit ticket: "Of all the things you learned in Unit 3, which TWO ideas were load-bearing for today's analysis? Why?"
48–50 min

• Marzano. Tomorrow is the transfer task.

Prep: 2026-XJ parameter card (mass, velocity, current distance); DART parameters card; whiteboard space for class compilation; calculator availability.

Talk moves on the compilation: when groups disagree on the Δv requirement, push: "Whose lead-time assumption made the biggest difference? What if we had MORE time?"

Differentiation: ELs — pre-built two-column table (deflection / impact); SWDs — station role-cards (who computes what); advanced — "propose a deflection strategy other than impact. Explain via impulse-momentum."

title: Day 14 · Phase 5 — Unit 3 transfer task
bodyHtml:
DAY 14 · Phase 5 — Assessment of learning

4 multi-part problems integrating momentum, impulse, conservation, collisions, and asteroid deflection.

TODAY'S LEARNING TARGETS

All Unit 3 targets, surfaced through transfer problems.

THE ASTEROID THREAD TODAY

The transfer task includes one asteroid-deflection problem (Δv given by DART-style impact, find lead-time required for Earth-radius offset) as the load-bearing Reasoning prompt.

Lesson Flow

Time What happens
0–5 min

• Brief reminder of the 4-dimension rubric (Identify / Calculate / Construct / Transfer). Lowest-dimension grading.
5–45 min

• Transfer task. Independent. 4 multi-part problems: (1) compute momentum + impulse for a baseball-bat collision; (2) ballistic-pendulum problem (perfectly inelastic + bullet velocity); (3) 2D billiards collision with both balls' final velocities; (4) asteroid deflection — given a Δv from a DART-style impact, how much lead time produces a 6400-km lateral shift?
45–48 min

• Final Marzano on the unit target list.
48–50 min

• Growth chart updated. Unit 4 begins tomorrow — energy, the OTHER conserved quantity.

Prep: transfer task printed; calculators; equation reference card; quiet testing environment; growth chart pages.

Differentiation on the transfer task: ELs — vocabulary glossary attached (momentum, impulse, elastic, inelastic, deflection); SWDs — option to verbal-explain dimension 4 (Transfer) of any problem; advanced — bonus prompt: "Given the energies involved, would Unit 4 say the impact is worse than the momentum analysis suggests? Why?"

Closing: Growth chart picked up tomorrow at start of Unit 4 — energy is the OTHER conserved quantity.
